decision: Kept full semantic identities in the symbol report while using source words for ordinary C names
why: Semantic identity is needed for deterministic compiler ownership, but it does not need to make normal C unreadable
result: Implementation compiles; focused proofs and snapshots remain open

decision: Limited temporary removal to pure structural values and one private read with one same-block sink and no intervening observable operation
why: Removing every temporary would reintroduce C evaluation-order, aliasing, failure, and lifetime bugs
result: Adversarial proof matrix in progress

decision: Added and ran a typed adversarial matrix for safe folding and required at least one real fixture load to be folded
why: A planner-only test could pass while production emission ignored the plan
result: All semantic and integration checks passed; suite now stops only at the intentionally stale naming snapshot

decision: Represented compiler-owned record-field reads as direct HxcIR field places instead of an address temporary followed by dereference
why: The record local is already stable storage; manufacturing a pointer preserved no semantic fact and made both HxcIR and C harder to read
result: Focused aggregate semantics reach only the intentionally stale snapshot; refreshed generated C remains to be measured

decision: Separated the registered Caxecraft snapshot validator from the full CI runner
why: The updater already renders twice for determinism; launching the full cold/warm/native matrix afterward caused a 300-second timeout without improving snapshot ownership
result: Python syntax and fixture-policy gates pass; the normal and full product runners remain registered for CI

decision: Added an exact generated-C readability snapshot and an executable upper budget
why: A qualitative claim would not prevent encoded names, digests, gotos, or temporary noise from returning
result: Measured 356 unique temporaries versus 971 before the change, maximum identifier length 50, and zero forbidden naming/control-flow categories; budget is 400

decision: Projected internal method names into native test-only headers instead of hard-coding compiler spellings
why: Semantic/native QA should survive a readability improvement without treating an internal C spelling as public ABI
result: Caxecraft split full native matrix, fresh split/unity quick lane, and project-layout O0/O2 differential all pass

decision: Refreshed only suites containing the old symbol algorithm, role prefixes, or hexadecimal guards
why: A full 30-suite rewrite would waste time and obscure which output classes changed
evidence: rg inventory; registered snapshot updater; focused owner validations
result: Four core contracts plus nine lowering owners and seven integration owners were updated; every completed validator passes after fixing stale v1 assumptions

decision: Made every cleanup-carrying terminator a hard value-coalescing barrier
why: Haxe computes return/throw values before edge cleanup; delaying a local read into the emitted return expression could become wrong as non-trivial cleanup is admitted
result: A return-edge cleanup adversarial case now requires materialization and the focused evaluation-order/native differential suite passes

decision: Kept readable generated locals and members behind the short hxc_ ownership prefix
why: The C preprocessor rewrites macros before C scope lookup, so bare bool, NULL, NAN, or arbitrary header macros can corrupt otherwise scoped names
result: Native standard-header collision probe added; refreshed snapshots and full gates remain open

decision: Refreshed and rechecked the 21 affected snapshot owners, then ran the complete four-shard toolchain gate
why: Readable naming changes touch many generated artifacts, so byte snapshots, native behavior, determinism, sanitizers, and policy all needed to agree
evidence: examples/caxecraft/expected/readability-metrics.json; npm run snapshots:check; npm run test:governance; npm run test:toolchain:parallel
result: All 38 toolchain commands and 70 governance tests passed; full Caxecraft O0/O2/ASan+UBSan passed

decision: Used the released Beads 1.0.4 client for this checkout instead of migrating the shared database
why: The installed 1.1.0 client expects schema 53, while release 1.0.4 owns exactly the checkout's schema 32; independently migrating a shared remote could fork it
evidence: Beads v1.0.4 release migration inventory; .beads/metadata.json; bd dolt pull
result: Pulled current remote state and updated the active issue without changing the shared schema

decision: Corrected stale architecture text found by the independent audit
why: Normative documentation still described schema 1, unconditional load temporaries, and an unprefixed example after the compiler contract had changed
result: Docs now describe schema 2, macro-safe readable prefixes, and the conservative coalescing boundary; stale-term scan and formatting pass

decision: Reran governance after the review-driven documentation correction
why: The final staged state must be checked rather than relying only on the earlier pre-review run
evidence: npm run test:governance; npm run security:gitleaks:staged; git diff --cached --check
result: All 70 governance tests, staged secret scan, and staged diff check passed

decision: Accepted the compiler change after the macro, coalescing, metric, and documentation audit
why: Fresh eyes had first found macro collisions and stale normative prose; both needed correction before closure
result: APPROVE with no remaining correctness blocker; retain the reproducible 356 metric and disclose that the legacy 971 baseline has no saved pre-change artifact

decision: Clarified the earlier snapshot-owner count as 21 exact owners
why: The earlier four-plus-nine-plus-seven wording totals 20 and did not accurately describe the final targeted set
result: The final set is 21 owners and every owner passed exact comparison; this row supersedes the row-9 category count

decision: Recorded the exact compatible Beads release provenance
why: A temporary older client is safe only when its binary release and schema match can be independently checked
result: Verified bd 1.0.4 commit ce242a879 and exactly 32 migration files before pulling; no schema migration ran

decision: Kept the 90-second Caxecraft timeout as a visible performance signal after a saturated-host retry failed
why: The repeat pre-commit run saw load averages above 49 on 12 cores and timed out only the first Caxecraft Haxe render; weakening the timeout would hide contention rather than improve the compiler
evidence: pre-commit toolchain output; examples/caxecraft/run.py:196; npm run test:native
result: Contracts, object lowering, semantic lowering, and native smoke passed; Caxecraft had already passed the complete matrix before the saturated retry, whose failure contained no semantic mismatch

decision: Accepted the fresh-eyes audit while disclosing that a different model family was unavailable
why: Both available reviewers identified themselves as GPT-5 Codex, so presenting either as cross-family would be false
result: APPROVE with process caveats: no transcript directory, no durable legacy-971 artifact, current metric counts unique names, and strict cross-family review remains unmet

decision: Reopened the readability bead and added a snapshot-owned native symbol projection
why: Hosted GCC and Clang replayed checked-in C without the generated symbol report, so the harness could no longer resolve the renamed outcome function
result: The exact native-only Clang replay, static-initialization suite, registered snapshot check, CI policy, fixture policy, and all 70 governance tests pass locally; hosted rerun remains pending

decision: Accepted the hosted-CI repair after a focused fresh-eyes review
why: The reviewer confirmed normal renders derive the projection from hxc.symbols.json and native replay resolves only the stable Haxe identity instead of guessing internal C text
evidence: focused generated-C review; python3 test/static_initialization/run.py --native-only --toolchain clang
result: APPROVE with no blocker; centralized the one duplicated source identity, while true GNU GCC remains hosted-only and strict cross-family review remains unavailable

decision: Rejected the project-wide unique-name counter as the final maintainability rubric
why: Repeated temporary names made 530 declaration lines appear as 356 unique identifiers, and the report omitted schema, ownership, source mapping, typed goto reasons, and per-function/file limits
result: Canonical corpus selected: Caxecraft split/unity, body lowering, and evaluation order; shared lexer-backed report in progress

decision: Selected three generated corpora plus small independent handwritten kernels
why: Body lowering proves name collisions and optional source lines; evaluation order proves required temporaries and both typed goto authorities; Caxecraft proves real split/unity scale without absorbing the later performance benchmark epic
result: Shared report/schema implementation delegated in non-overlapping files; semantic/native gates remain overriding

decision: Made every admitted synthetic goto carry a typed control-flow-plan reason
why: Label spelling cannot prove that a jump is justified; the validated plan must own its category and exact emitted occurrence
result: Schema-3 report reconciles one loop-switch escape and six irreducible jumps; focused Eval/native suite passes

decision: Counted only true hxc_tmp_ declarations and kept casts, arguments, and returns as references
why: The first lexer version treated several uses as declarations, which would freeze a false 578 baseline
result: Both methods now find exactly 514 declarations; the report records 757 references, 262 declarations per 1000 full-corpus function lines, and zero gotos

decision: Added durable handwritten ordered-call and structured-control-flow kernels
why: A readability budget must not reward deleting locals that a C programmer needs to preserve Haxe evaluation order
result: Governance generates and schema-validates both reports; each stays within its reviewed policy with no compiler temporaries or gotos

decision: Snapshot-owned both Caxecraft layouts and all generated-corpus maintainability reports
why: Exact C bytes, policy metrics, typed goto provenance, and split/unity parity must fail together on unexplained regression
evidence: registered snapshot updater; body-lowering, evaluation-order, and Caxecraft validation runners
result: Caxecraft split/unity report 53 identical functions, 514 declarations, 757 references, 10000-bp identity mapping, 7735-bp spans, and zero gotos; evaluation source-span floor raised to 9000 bp

decision: Recomputed checked-in Caxecraft reports from the exact C bytes in native-only validation
why: Hashes bind a report to inputs but do not independently prove that its recorded metrics came from those inputs
result: Both checked-in layouts re-lex, reconstruct typed ownership/function mappings, and reproduce their complete reports exactly

decision: Separated process isolation from resource contention when interpreting local timeouts
why: Sibling-repository jobs cannot change isolated outputs but can consume CPU, memory, and disk enough to cross a fixed wall-clock timeout
evidence: AGENTS.md; docs/test-performance.md; observed saturated-host process/load evidence
result: Agents must preserve diagnostics and timeouts, retry under representative load before classifying a regression, and never use saturated-host wall time as performance evidence

decision: Bounded temporary references independently from temporary declarations
why: One generated temporary could otherwise be reused many times while declaration density stayed unchanged
result: Caxecraft records 757 references under an 800 ceiling; a focused failure proves the first offending source coordinate is reported

decision: Compared split and unity maintainability semantics directly
why: Both layouts share one semantic plan, so source-backed function complexity and aggregate name, temporary, branch, and goto metrics must not drift independently
evidence: examples/caxecraft/run.py; checked-in split and unity reports
result: All 53 aggregate function metrics and all 41 source-backed function projections agree; only artifact layout and coordinates differ

decision: Centralized exact-C report replay in the shared analyzer and restored every preserved typed goto authority
why: Caxecraft-specific replay duplicated closed report decoding, while a generic replay that dropped goto provenance could not reproduce an admitted-jump corpus
result: Exact bytes now reconstruct ownership, source/function mappings, symbol explanations, and typed jump categories; focused no-jump and two-jump replay cases pass

decision: Extended collision-suffix recognition through the compiler's full 12-to-64-hex contract and enforced checked-in layout parity in native-only validation
why: Fresh review found that 20-to-64-character collision suffixes could evade the hash budget and that independently valid split/unity reports could diverge in the Haxe-free path
result: A 20-character suffix regression is classified and explained, and every Caxecraft path now compares the shared semantic maintainability projection

decision: Ran the complete canonical matrix and then reran every suite affected by final review fixes
why: The rubric changes cross Python QA, typed Haxe provenance, exact C snapshots, native execution, sanitizers, and governance, so one narrow test cannot establish closure
evidence: npm run test:toolchain:parallel; npm run test:caxecraft-domain; npm run test:body-lowering; npm run test:evaluation-order; npm run test:governance; npm run format:haxe:check
result: All 38 toolchain commands, full and quick Caxecraft lanes, focused lowering suites, 88 governance tests, formatter, snapshot catalog, schemas, and exact checked-in report replay pass

decision: Accepted the stable maintainability-rubric diff after two review-driven corrections
why: Fresh review independently traced schema, policy, replay, goto provenance, suffix classification, split/unity parity, snapshots, bounded claims, and the append-only trail
evidence: fresh final generated-C maintainability review; focused regression reruns
result: APPROVE with no remaining blocker; reviewer identified as GPT-5 Codex family, so strict different-family review remains unavailable and is not claimed
